title: Indentation and Blocks
summary: Understand why indentation controls structure in Python.

sketchBlocks.title: Why indentation matters
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
In Python, indentation is not just for style. It tells Python which lines belong inside a block.

A block is a group of lines that run together after a statement like `if`, `elif`, or `else`.

```python
age = 16

if age >= 18:
    print("adult")
print("done")
```

Step by step:
- `if age >= 18:` checks a condition.
- The indented line under it belongs to the `if` block.
- `print("done")` is not indented, so it is outside the block.
- Because `16 >= 18` is false, Python skips `print("adult")` and still runs `print("done")`.

Worked example output:

```python
done
```

If you indent a line, you are saying, "This line is part of that block."

Try it yourself: Type the example in the code editor, click Run, then change `age` to `20` and check how the output panel changes.

sketchBlocks.title: Lines with the same indentation belong together
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Inside a block, lines that have the same indentation level are grouped together.

```python
score = 85

if score >= 80:
    print("Great job")
    print("You passed the target")
print("Check complete")
```

Line by line:
- `if score >= 80:` starts a conditional block.
- The next two lines are indented the same amount, so both are inside the `if` block.
- `print("Check complete")` is back at the left side, so it runs after the block.

If `score` is `85`, the output is:

```python
Great job
You passed the target
Check complete
```

If `score` were `60`, the first two lines would be skipped, but `Check complete` would still print.

Your turn: Change `score` to `60`, click Run, and compare the output panel with the earlier result.

sketchBlocks.title: Each branch has its own block
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
With `if`, `elif`, and `else`, each branch can have its own indented block.

```python
number = 0

if number > 0:
    print("positive")
elif number < 0:
    print("negative")
else:
    print("zero")
```

How this works:
- `if number > 0:` checks the first condition.
- `elif number < 0:` is checked only if the first condition is false.
- `else:` runs if the earlier conditions are false.
- The indented line under each branch belongs only to that branch.

For `number = 0`, the output is:

```python
zero
```

A common beginner mistake is forgetting to indent the line after `if` or `else`. Python needs that indentation to know what should run in that branch.

Try this: Change `number` to `7` and then to `-3`. Click Run each time and watch which block runs.

quizDraft.title: What indentation does
quizDraft.prompt: In Python, what does indentation mainly tell the program?
quizDraft.hint: Think about which lines belong under an `if` or `else`.

help.concept: Python uses indentation to define blocks of code. A block is the set of lines that belong to a statement like `if`, `elif`, or `else`.
help.hint_1: Look for the choice about grouping lines under a conditional branch.
help.hint_2: It is not mainly about decoration or comments. It controls structure.

options: Which lines belong to the same block
How many times a line will run
Whether print adds a new line

quizDraft.title: Line outside the block
quizDraft.prompt: What is true about a line that is not indented under an `if` statement?
quizDraft.hint: Compare a line aligned with `if` to a line indented under it.

help.concept: A line aligned with the `if` statement is outside that `if` block, so it is not controlled by the condition in the same way as indented lines are.
help.hint_1: A line at the left edge is separate from the indented branch.
help.hint_2: If the condition is false, the outside line can still run because it is not part of the skipped block.

quizDraft.title: Choose all lines in the block
quizDraft.prompt:
Consider this code:

```python
if True:
    print("A")
    print("B")
print("C")
```

Which lines are inside the `if` block? Choose all correct answers.
quizDraft.hint: Only the indented lines under `if True:` belong to that block.

help.concept: In Python, all lines directly under a statement and indented the same amount are part of that block.
help.hint_1: Check which lines are shifted to the right under `if True:`.
help.hint_2: The two `print` lines with matching indentation are grouped together; the left-aligned line is separate.

quizDraft.title: Valid ideas about blocks
quizDraft.prompt: Which statements about Python blocks are correct? Choose all correct answers.
quizDraft.hint: Think about indentation level and how `if`/`elif`/`else` branches are grouped.

help.concept: Blocks in Python are formed by indentation. Each branch can have its own block, and matching indentation groups lines together.
help.hint_1: Look for statements about same indentation and separate branch bodies.
help.hint_2: Reject any choice that says indentation is only visual or optional.

options: Lines with the same indentation under an `if` can belong to the same block
An `else` branch can have its own indented block
Indentation in Python is only for readability and does not affect execution
A line outside the block must be indented more than the `if` line

quizDraft.title: Build a simple if-else
quizDraft.prompt: Arrange the tokens to form a valid Python `if`/`else` structure that prints `yes` when `x > 0` and `no` otherwise.
quizDraft.hint: Start with the `if` line, then its indented body, then `else:`, then its indented body.

help.concept: A valid conditional structure needs the branch headers in order and the indented body line directly under each header.
help.hint_1: The condition line must come before any indented `print` line.
help.hint_2: After the first indented body, place `else:` at the original indentation level, then its own indented print.

quizDraft.title: Arrange a two-line block
quizDraft.prompt: Arrange the tokens to make a valid program where both messages are inside the `if` block.
quizDraft.hint: Both `print` lines should stay under the `if` line with the same indentation.

help.concept: Multiple lines can belong to one block when they are indented equally under the same header line.
help.hint_1: Put the `if` header first, then the two indented lines.
help.hint_2: The two `print` lines should be consecutive and both shifted right the same amount.

quizDraft.title: Complete the block header
quizDraft.prompt: Choose the missing word to complete the conditional line.
quizDraft.hint: A conditional branch starts with Python's keyword for checking a condition.

help.concept: Python starts a basic conditional with the `if` keyword, followed by a condition and a colon.
help.hint_1: You use this keyword before a condition like `x > 3`.
help.hint_2: It is the first branch keyword, not the fallback branch.

quizDraft.title: Choose the indented line
quizDraft.prompt: Pick the line that correctly fills the blank so the print statement is inside the `if` block.
quizDraft.hint: The line inside the block must be indented.

help.concept: A statement belongs to an `if` block only when it is indented under the `if` line.
help.hint_1: Look for the choice with spaces before `print`.
help.hint_2: The correct line is not left-aligned; it is shifted right to show block membership.

quizDraft.title: Print from an if block
quizDraft.prompt: Write Python code that reads one integer. If the number is greater than 10, print `big`. Otherwise print `small`.
quizDraft.hint: Read the number first, then use an `if` with an `else`.

help.concept: An `if` block runs when its condition is true, and an `else` block handles the other case. Indentation places each `print` in the correct branch.
help.hint_1: Convert the input to an integer before comparing it with 10.
help.hint_2: You need one print for the greater-than case and one print for the remaining case.

quizDraft.starterCode:
n = int(input())

# TODO: print "big" if n > 10, otherwise print "small"

quizDraft.title: Use two lines in one block
quizDraft.prompt: Write Python code that reads one integer. If the number is at least 5, print `high` on one line and `checked` on the next line. Otherwise print only `low`.
quizDraft.hint: The true branch needs two indented print lines.

help.concept: A single branch can contain multiple statements when they share the same indentation under the branch header.
help.hint_1: Use `if n >= 5:` and place two print statements under it.
help.hint_2: The `else` branch has one indented print line, while the `if` branch has two.

quizDraft.starterCode:
n = int(input())

# TODO: write the conditional output

quizDraft.title: Classify with if elif else
quizDraft.prompt: Write Python code that reads one integer. Print `positive` if it is greater than 0, `negative` if it is less than 0, and `zero` otherwise.
quizDraft.hint: This needs three cases, so `elif` fits well.

help.concept: When there are several exclusive cases, `if`, `elif`, and `else` let you place each result in its own block.
help.hint_1: Check the greater-than-zero case first, then the less-than-zero case.
help.hint_2: The final branch should handle the remaining value after the first two comparisons fail.

quizDraft.starterCode:
n = int(input())

# TODO: print positive, negative, or zero

quizDraft.title: Check the special case first
quizDraft.prompt: Write Python code that reads one integer score. Print `perfect` if the score is exactly 100. Otherwise, if the score is at least 60, print `pass`. In all other cases, print `retry`.
quizDraft.hint: Handle the exact 100 case before the broader passing range.

help.concept: When one case is more specific than another, checking the special case first prevents it from being absorbed by a broader earlier condition.
help.hint_1: Use `if score == 100:` before checking whether the score is at least 60.
help.hint_2: After the exact-match branch, the next branch should cover the remaining passing scores.

quizDraft.starterCode:
score = int(input())

# TODO: print perfect, pass, or retry

quizDraft.title: Use an if inside another if
quizDraft.prompt: Write Python code that reads one integer age. If the age is at least 13, then check one more condition: if the age is at least 18, print `adult`; otherwise print `teen`. If the age is less than 13, print `child`.
quizDraft.hint: Start with the outer age check, then place another `if` inside that block.

help.concept: Nested conditionals use one indented block inside another. The inner `if` only runs when the outer condition is true.
help.hint_1: First separate ages below 13 from ages 13 and above.
help.hint_2: Inside the outer true branch, compare again to decide between the two older groups.

quizDraft.starterCode:
age = int(input())

# TODO: use a nested if to print child, teen, or adult

projectDraft.title: Practice with indentation and conditional blocks
